content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Members of the Committee

Date : April 11, 1980

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Alan W. Courtney [signature]

Subject: Requirement of a Public Hearing in Amending a Conflict of
         Interest Code

The Air Resources Board by its memo of March 11, 1980,
has raised the contention that amendments to the Conflict
of Interest Code are regulations "prescribing an agency's
organization or procedure" and are therefore exempt from
the notice and hearing requirements of the Administrative
Procedure Act. See: Govt.C. §11421(a).

While no cases could be found which interpret this
section of the Government Code, two cases were located
which interpret a similar provision contained at §11371(b)
of the Government Code. This statute exempts from the term
"regulation" any rule "which relates only to the internal
management of the state agencies." The effect of this
provision is to exempt internal management rules from the
notice and hearing requirements of the APA. The analogy
between internal management rules and regulations
prescribing an agency's organization or procedure is
sufficiently strong enough to consider the cases interpreting
§11371(b) as being persuasive on the issue raised by the ARB.

Poschman v. Dumke (1973) 31 C.A.3d 932, 107 Cal.Rptr.
596, dealt with an attempted revocation by the Board of
Trustees of the California State Colleges of its "policy on
Grievance Procedures for Academic Personnel." The regulation
was attacked by an assistant professor who was denied tenure.
The assistant professor argued that the regulation was
invalid because notice and hearing requirements were not met.
The Board of Trustees agreed that there had been no public
hearing, but countered by asserting that the regulation
related only to the internal management of a state agency,
thus falling within the Government Code exception. The
Court of Appeal rejected the argument advanced by the Board
of Trustees and found that "the better reasoned view is to
regard 'internal management' narrowly so as to encompass
accounting techniques and the like.(Citations omitted.)

Tenure within any school system is a matter of serious
consequence involving an important public interest. The
consequences are not solely confined to school administra-
tion or affect only the academic community." 31 C.A.3d
at 942-943.

In Armistead v. State Personnel Board (1978) 22 Cal.
3d 198, 149 Cal.Rptr. 1, 583 P.2d 744, the California
Supreme Court unanimously rejected the State Personnel
Board's contention that a regulation concerning termination
of employment was a rule governing the board's internal
affairs. The Court stated that the board had "confused
the internal rules which may govern the department's
procedure...and the rules necessary to properly consider
the interests of all...under the...statutes..." 22 Cal.
3d at 203-204.

From the foregoing it is concluded that the argument
of the ARB should be rejected because the amendment of a
Conflict of Interest Code is fundamentally different from
the adoption of accounting techniques and because the
consequences are not solely confined to the ARB. The
amended Conflict of Interest Code will not only affect the
public's right to know, but also impacts upon the employees
of the ARB who are designated in that they will be exposed
to civil, criminal or administrative liability, as well as
dismissal, if they violate provisions of the Conflict of
Interest Code. As termination of employment is a distinct
result of a violation, the holding of Armistead would
indicate that public hearing and all other procedural
requirements must be met.

It is axiomatic that a regulation not adopted in
conformity with the APA is invalid. City of San Marcos v.
California Highway Commission (1976) 66 C.A.3d 183, 131
Cal.Rptr. 804. Thus, if the position asserted by the ARB
is accepted, the later enforcement of the Code would be
subject to a procedural challenge by the defendant. Unless
the ARB is able to adequately support its contentions, the
saving of effort by the ARB may well undermine the validity
of the amended Conflict of Interest Code.

Finally, the argument advanced by the ARB that the
process of amending and reviewing the Code is duplicative
should be rejected outright. The Political Reform Act is
structured so that two independent procedural requirements
must be met in order for a Code to be validly adopted. It
is not for the ARB to decide what procedures will adequately
safeguard the public's interest,as the Political Reform Act
has already made that determination.
